glossary.def: An option price approximated as the discounted average of its payoff over many simulated futures, V̂ = e^{-rT}·mean(payoff). It is unbiased for any number of paths, so its only error is statistical noise, and its per-path cost is independent of how complex the payoff is.

The standard deviation of the Monte Carlo estimate itself, SE = σ_Y/√N, where σ_Y is the spread of the discounted payoffs and N the number of paths. It is estimated from the same run that estimates the price and sets the 95% confidence interval at ±1.96·SE.

The defining and unforgiving rate of plain Monte Carlo: error falls as the reciprocal square root of the sample size. Halving the error costs 4× the paths and gaining one decimal digit costs 100×, which is why variance reduction matters more than raw compute.

Any technique that shrinks the payoff spread σ_Y rather than growing N, buying accuracy almost for free. The main families are antithetic variates, control variates, importance sampling, and low-discrepancy (quasi-random) sequences.

Pairing each Gaussian shock Z with its mirror −Z and averaging the two payoffs into one sample. For a payoff monotone in Z the pair is negatively correlated, so variance falls by a factor (1+ρ) with ρ<0; for non-monotone payoffs it can backfire.

Subtracting a correlated quantity X with a known mean, Y_cv = Y − c(X − E[X]), leaving the price unchanged but cutting variance by a factor (1−ρ²). A control 0.99-correlated with the payoff reduces variance roughly 50×; the geometric-Asian price is the classic control for the arithmetic Asian.

A derivative whose payoff depends on the whole trajectory of the underlying, not just its final value — e.g. an Asian (average price) or a barrier (knock-in/knock-out at a level). These rarely admit closed forms, making them the natural domain of Monte Carlo, but they require simulating full paths and incur a discretization bias from the time step.

Two ways to get sensitivities from a simulation. Bump-and-revalue takes a finite difference of two re-priced scenarios and only works reliably with common random numbers; the pathwise (infinitesimal-perturbation) estimator differentiates the payoff itself for an unbiased, single-pass Greek when the payoff is smooth enough.

refs.cite: Boyle, P. (1977). Options: A Monte Carlo Approach. Journal of Financial Economics 4(3).
Glasserman, P. (2003). Monte Carlo Methods in Financial Engineering. Springer.
Boyle, P., Broadie, M. & Glasserman, P. (1997). Monte Carlo Methods for Security Pricing. Journal of Economic Dynamics and Control 21.
Longstaff, F. & Schwartz, E. (2001). Valuing American Options by Simulation: A Simple Least-Squares Approach. Review of Financial Studies 14(1).
Giles, M. & Glasserman, P. (2006). Smoking Adjoints: Fast Monte Carlo Greeks. RISK Magazine.
Kloeden, P. & Platen, E. (1992). Numerical Solution of Stochastic Differential Equations. Springer.
